preamble: In the evolution of East Asian tonal systems, the Voice Loss of initial consonants like [b] to [p] is a primary catalyst for a Register Split. Voiced initials originally lowered the Pitch Contour of the following vowel (Low Register), while voiceless initials raised it (High Register). When the voicing distinction was lost, the pitch difference was phonemicized as distinct tones.
phoneticEffects: Voice Loss, Devoicing, Register Split, Tonogenesis

examples.note: The loss of voicing in initial consonants led to the doubling of the tone system (from 3 to 6 tones) in Vietnamese, creating low-register counterparts.
Voiced initials like [b] in Early Middle Chinese (Quánzhuó) triggered a split in the four traditional tone categories (Yīn/Yáng split).
Tai-Kadai languages underwent a massive Register Split where the voicing of the initial consonant determined the height of the resulting tone.

sources: Haudricourt, A. G. (1954). De l'origine des tons en viêtnamien.
Pittayaporn, P. (2009). The Phonology of Proto-Tai.
